---
title: "Environment Markers"
tagline: "Or what to Do When the Dependency Depends on the Machine"
type: scenario
tool: python
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When the Dependency Depends on the Machine*
No JVM sibling. A Python requirement can carry a **marker** (PEP 508) — a condition on the *installing environment* that decides whether the dependency applies at all. `demo-lib-c; python_version >= "3.8"` installs `demo-lib-c` only on new-enough Python. The resolved graph becomes a function of the machine.

::: {.callout-note title="Tool Versions"}
Tested with **pip 25.3**, **Poetry 2.4**, **uv 0.11**. Markers are PEP 508, a standard — identical behaviour across all tools and versions.
:::

#### ! Example

```{.text include="pip/requirements.txt" snippet="p8-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
Both listed dependencies install; a requirements file is a flat list.

#### ! Actual
Only the dependency whose marker is *true on this machine* installs. On Python ≥ 3.8, `demo-lib-c` installs and `demo-lib-d` is skipped entirely. Run the identical file on Python 3.7 and the result flips. Same declaration, different environment — legitimately, by design.

#### ! Why
A marker is evaluated against the target interpreter's own facts: `python_version`, `sys_platform`, `platform_machine`, `os_name`, and more. The dependency isn't "included then filtered" — it is included *conditionally*, and the condition is data attached to the requirement. This is how one wheel's metadata says "I need `pywin32` on Windows and nothing on Linux" without shipping two packages. The JVM has no equivalent; the closest is separate classifier artifacts, chosen by hand.

#### ! Fix
*   **Platform-specific deps:** guard them with `; sys_platform == "win32"` (or `== "darwin"`, `== "linux"`).
*   **Version-gated backports:** `importlib-metadata; python_version < "3.10"` — pull the backport only where the stdlib lacks it.
*   **Debugging "it resolved differently on CI":** print the markers. CI on Linux and a laptop on macOS legitimately resolve different graphs.

#### ! Risk
* LOW A well-scoped platform or version marker.
* MEDIUM Assuming your lock file is universal — a lock resolved on macOS may omit a Windows-only, marker-gated dependency (uv's cross-platform lock handles this; `pip freeze` does not).
* HIGH Testing only on the platform where a marker excludes a fragile dependency, then shipping to the platform where it's included and untested.

#### ! Check

**Fast check (on Python ≥ 3.8):**

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
pip show demo-lib-c   # present  (marker python_version >= "3.8" is true)
pip show demo-lib-d   # absent   (marker python_version < "3.8" is false)
```

**What to look for:** exactly one of the two packages present, selected by the interpreter's version.

**Conclusion:** Markers make the dependency set conditional on the environment.

#### ! Takeaway
A Python requirement can be conditional. The same file resolves to different environments across Python versions and platforms — a feature for portability, a trap for anyone who assumes one lock fits all machines.

#### ! Related
*   [P5](../scenario-5-locks/guide.qmd) - Why a single-platform lock can miss marker-gated deps.
*   [P10](../scenario-10-venv/guide.qmd) - Environment isolation, the other half of "works on my machine".

#### ! Wild
Environment markers are why `pywin32` doesn't wreck your Linux CI. A cross-platform library lists it as `pywin32; sys_platform == "win32"`, and every non-Windows install silently skips a dependency that wouldn't even build there. It is one of packaging's quietest successes — invisible precisely because it works.
